text: Clean Code by Robert C. Martin defines clean code as code that is easy to read, understand, and change. The core premise is that code is read far more often than it is written — so optimizing for readability is optimizing for maintenance cost. A professional developer writes code for the next person who reads it, not just for the compiler.

Clean Code — Meaningful Names: variable, function, and class names should reveal intent. Avoid abbreviations, single letters (except loop counters), and misleading names. A name should answer: why it exists, what it does, and how it is used. If a name requires a comment to explain it, the name is wrong.

Clean Code — Functions: functions should do one thing, do it well, and do it only. The ideal function has zero arguments — each argument adds cognitive load. Functions should be small, have no side effects, and either do something or answer something, never both. Deeply nested conditionals signal a function doing too much.

Clean Code — Comments: the best comment is no comment — write code that explains itself. Comments lie over time because code changes but comments do not always follow. Acceptable comments: legal notices, explanation of intent behind an unusual decision, warnings of consequences, TODO markers. Unacceptable: comments that restate what the code already says.

Clean Code — Error Handling: use exceptions rather than return codes. Do not return null — it forces callers to check everywhere and one missed check causes a NullPointerException. Do not pass null to functions. Write try-catch-finally first when writing code that can throw — define the scope of error handling before the happy path.

Clean Code — The Boy Scout Rule: leave the code cleaner than you found it. Every time you touch a file, improve something small — rename a variable, break up a long function, remove a dead comment. Continuous small improvements prevent code from rotting over time. Professional responsibility includes caring about the codebase beyond your own ticket.
